a: The NAHB AI figures cover single-family home builders rather than a remodeling-only sample.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a median harmonization factor, which may not represent the narrowed for-others subset.

rho: Custom projects and undocumented site conditions make remodeling less standardized than production homebuilding.

s5: It is not construction-specific or restricted to $1–10M normalized EBITDA.
s5: Deaths without transferable operations and internal reorganizations are excluded from the target construct.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers outsource responsibility for a residential project or maintenance program. Revenue is commonly earned through fixed-price, time-and-materials, cost-plus, design-build, construction-management, insurance-restoration, or recurring maintenance arrangements; repeatability is strongest with property managers, multifamily owners, insurers, and prior homeowners.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition includes for-sale remodelers alongside contractors and construction managers. The lens is narrowed to coherent for-others service businesses because own-account resale activity does not have an external service customer; this is a business-model distinction, not an attractiveness choice.
lens.excluded_activities: For-sale remodeling performed on the firm's own account; captive internal construction units; non-control financing vehicles; nonresidential remodeling; new-home construction; and firms whose primary activity is a specialized trade classified in NAICS 238.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market residential remodeling general contractors, design-build firms, and project construction managers providing additions, alterations, reconstruction, maintenance, and repairs to external homeowners, landlords, multifamily owners, property managers, insurers, and housing organizations under recurring or repeat project relationships.

caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 2361 residential building construction, not remodelers alone.
The NAHB AI figures cover single-family home builders rather than a remodeling-only sample.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a median harmonization factor, which may not represent the narrowed for-others subset.
a.change_evidence: A 236118-only payroll roster mapped to SOC codes, plus time-and-motion data across estimating, design, project management, sales, administration, warranty, and field trades and an audit of automation already in use, would materially move this estimate.
a.rationale: Residential-building employment is led by carpenters, laborers, trade supervisors, and construction managers, while project-management, office, bookkeeping, sales, and administrative roles are also material. AI can substitute portions of lead qualification, scope drafting, estimating, visualization, scheduling, document control, purchasing, customer updates, change-order administration, and finance; it has limited direct reach into demolition, installation, site measurement, inspection, and physical repair. The range is a wage-weighted judgment rather than an observed task-exposure share.

d5.bridge: Harvard's LIRA covers nominal U.S. improvement and maintenance spending on owner-occupied homes and forecasts year-over-year growth slowing to 1.6% by end-2026, while NAHB reports 24 consecutive quarters of remodeling sentiment above 50 and structural tailwinds from aging homes, mortgage lock-in, and aging in place. I convert these broader nominal spending and sentiment indicators into a near-flat-to-modestly-growing five-year constant-price, constant-quality quantity base for professional service firms. Inflation can make nominal growth overstate quantity; the owner-occupied focus omits rental/multifamily work, while the narrowed lens omits DIY and own-account activity.
caveats: The explicit LIRA forecast extends only four quarters and is nominal, requiring extrapolation and inflation adjustment judgment.
NAHB sentiment is a member survey and is not a quantity index.
National housing-age evidence masks large regional differences in incomes, insurance, disasters, regulation, and home values.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar forecast through 2031 split by professionally installed versus DIY work, owner-occupied versus rental/multifamily properties, project type, and buyer segment, with transaction volumes, permits, insurance restoration, energy incentives, and backlog data, would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: A 41-year median age for owner-occupied homes, replacement cycles, accessibility, energy, and resilience needs support durable work. High financing and labor costs, affordability pressure, weak home turnover, discretionary-project deferral, and DIY substitution restrain real quantity. The result is a wide but modest-growth-centered five-year range.

caveats: No source quantifies the share of 236118 LMM firms that are for-others, repeat-service operators.
Some firms may be project businesses dependent on one owner even at modeled LMM EBITDA.
The frozen n is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE using a homebuilding sector margin rather than observed remodeler EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of the 2,921 frozen LMM-band firms using tax NAICS, websites, customer concentration, repeat-revenue records, contract types, own-account resale revenue, and organizational depth would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Census explicitly includes remodeling general contractors, design-build firms, and project construction managers, which ordinarily provide outsourced work to external property owners. The main exclusions are own-account for-sale remodelers, firms with insufficient repeat-service characteristics, captive activity, and miscoded specialty trades. Because the frozen count already targets the LMM EBITDA band, most correctly classified firms at that scale should have an operating organization and repeat or recurring customer flow.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 236118 ($28,357,973,829) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 236118 ($142,679,238,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1414 (margins.json[236]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Homebuilding EBITDA/Sales 14.14%); revenue/employee $308,369 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 236118); class <5: 108,299 firms, avg revenue/firm $413,317 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,443 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 15,331 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,837,261 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $259,789 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 6,116 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,793,668 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $536,425 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,753 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,345,278 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,604,222 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 168 firms, avg revenue/firm $54,315,685 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,680,238 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $140,674,682 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,891,400 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,921

caveats: The 39% DIY statistic covers all projects, not the current professional LMM service basket.
Accountable-operator persistence is inferred from workflow and liability structure rather than directly measured.
The estimate preserves the operator role, not today's staffing levels or administrative tasks.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal American Housing Survey data on professional versus DIY project shares by project value and complexity, plus platform data showing the share of remodeling quantity completed through owner-direct trade orchestration without a general contractor or project manager, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: The current professional service basket requires a party accountable for site assessment, scope, permits, sequencing, subcontractors, safety, occupied-home protection, quality, change orders, and warranty. Software can enable visualization, quoting, self-service selections, and direct trade matching, while some projects can shift to DIY; Census reported that 39% of all home projects were DIY in 2021. Yet AI cannot physically alter or repair a home, and the complex projects served by LMM remodelers continue to require an accountable operator.

caveats: No direct source measures five-year retention of AI-generated gross benefits in residential remodeling.
Materials inflation, project mix, homeowner selections, and change orders can mask true pass-through.
Implementation difficulty and demand changes are excluded here and handled in rho and d5/o.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level analysis separating fixed-price, cost-plus, time-and-materials, and maintenance agreements, paired with pre/post-automation gross margins, estimate accuracy, change orders, discounts, bid win rates, repeat-customer pricing, and renewal terms, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Fixed-price and design-build projects can retain workflow savings until competitors reprice, whereas time-and-materials or transparent cost-plus arrangements pass through avoided billable hours more directly. Over five years, recurring commercial buyers will rebid and homeowners can compare quotes, but reputation, design quality, trust, responsiveness, and local capacity prevent full competitive pass-through. The discounted retained share is therefore centered slightly above one-half.

caveats: AI use is not equivalent to successful labor implementation.
Custom projects and undocumented site conditions make remodeling less standardized than production homebuilding.
Implementation could be higher for scaled design-build platforms and lower for owner-led local contractors.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month before-and-after records from at least 25 LMM remodelers deploying AI across estimating, design, scheduling, procurement, customer communication, and administration—measuring hours, estimate error, change orders, rework, cycle time, and net headcount avoidance—would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be feasible in digital sales and back-office workflows, but remodeling is fragmented and each existing home presents uncertain conditions, local-code issues, homeowner choices, change orders, and coordination exceptions. NAHB's adjacent builder evidence showed AI adoption concentrated in marketing and market planning, while Census found lower use among the smallest firms. Quality, liability, weak data integration, and change management limit conversion of exposed tasks into avoided labor.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 U.S. small-business-owner survey reports five-year sale, gift, or rare offering plans for 17% of owners age 55 or older and 10% of younger owners; Census data cited there indicate that 52.3% of employer firms have owners age 55 or older. I translate this into an approximately 14% qualifying-transfer probability and widen the range. The source population is all U.S. small-business owners, not LMM residential remodelers; intentions and gifts overstate closed qualifying control deals, while distress or unplanned exits may cause understatement.
caveats: The survey measures plans, not completed control transfers, and combines sales with gifts and rare offerings.
It is not construction-specific or restricted to $1–10M normalized EBITDA.
Deaths without transferable operations and internal reorganizations are excluded from the target construct.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-control transaction data for U.S. 236118 firms over at least five years, linked to EBITDA, seller age, ownership structure, buyer type, transaction form, licensing, and post-close continuity from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA lenders, and specialist brokers would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: Remodeling is fragmented and often owner-led, supporting succession supply but also causing attempted transitions to become closures, asset sales, or nontransferable customer-book losses. LMM scale improves organizational transferability relative to a typical microbusiness, but project liabilities, licensing, reputation, and owner relationships remain material.

principal_driver: A durable installed base of aging homes creates repeat project demand while AI can compress the coordination and administrative layer around field work.
principal_weakness: Physical trades dominate delivery, and fragmented custom projects with concealed conditions constrain both AI implementation and operational standardization.
